featuretablefilter 0.1.0
  • Get started
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • featuretablefilter Tutorial: Complete Guide to Microbiome Feature Table Filtering

    Calculate pairwise mutual information between features

    compute_mutual_information.Rd

    Computes the mutual information (MI) matrix for all feature pairs, measuring the amount of information obtained about one feature through the other. High MI indicates strong co-occurrence or mutual exclusion patterns.

    compute_mutual_information(
  abundances,
  k = 3,
  bias_correction = TRUE,
  min_prevalence = 0.05
)

    Arguments

    abundances

    Numeric matrix or data.frame of feature abundances (rows = features, columns = samples).

    k

    Number of nearest neighbors to use for MI estimation. Default is 3. Higher values give smoother estimates but may miss local patterns.

    bias_correction

    Logical. Apply bias correction to MI estimates. Default is TRUE (recommended for small sample sizes).

    min_prevalence

    Minimum proportion of samples where a feature must be present (non-zero) to be included in MI calculation. Default is 0.05 (5 computational burden and focus on meaningful signals.

    Value

    A symmetric matrix of mutual information values between features. Diagonal elements are NA (self-information not computed).

    Details

    This function uses a k-nearest neighbors approach for continuous data, which is more robust than histogram-based methods for sparse microbiome data.

    Examples

    # Create example feature table
# mat <- matrix(rpois(100 * 20, lambda = 5), nrow = 100, ncol = 20)
# mi_matrix <- compute_mutual_information(mat)

# Use with prevalence filtering
# filtered_mat <- table[table[, -1] > 0][rowSums(table[, -1] > 0) / ncol(table) > 0.05, ]
# mi_matrix <- compute_mutual_information(as.matrix(filtered_mat[, -1]))
